name: One World Government
description: The "One World Government" (OWG) is a conspiracy theory positing a secret, totalitarian global power structure. Proponents believe a cabal of elites is working to dismantle national sovereignty and establish a single, authoritarian government. This government would control all aspects of human life, including finances, resources, and individual freedoms. Origins are often traced back to historical secret societies like the Illuminati and Freemasons, with claims of their continued influence through modern organizations such as the United Nations, the World Bank, and the World Economic Forum. Evidence cited includes international treaties, global financial institutions, and alleged secret meetings of world leaders. Mainstream perspectives reject the OWG as a baseless conspiracy theory, citing a lack of credible evidence and highlighting the benefits of international cooperation and diplomacy. Critics argue that proponents misinterpret legitimate efforts at global governance, such as international law and trade agreements, as evidence of a sinister plot. They also point out the diversity of interests and ideologies among nations, making a unified, totalitarian world government highly improbable. The theory often overlaps with anti-globalization sentiments and fears about the erosion of national identity and cultural diversity. Many believe the OWG is a modern manifestation of anxieties about centralized power and loss of control in an increasingly interconnected world. Scholarly analysis often frames the OWG as a symptom of distrust in institutions and a reflection of broader societal anxieties about globalization and technological advancement. The theory also intersects with religious and political ideologies, often incorporating apocalyptic narratives or fears about a "New World Order."
subtitle: Secret plot for global domination?

claims.claim: A secret cabal controls global events.
claims.evidence: Meetings of organizations like the Bilderberg Group and the Trilateral Commission are cited as evidence of secret decision-making.
claims.criticism: These meetings are public knowledge and involve discussions on global issues, not secret conspiracies. Transparency is often enforced.

claims.claim: National sovereignty is being eroded by international organizations.
claims.evidence: International treaties and agreements are seen as a surrender of national control to supranational bodies.
claims.criticism: International cooperation is necessary to address global challenges like climate change, pandemics, and economic crises. Treaties are negotiated and ratified by sovereign nations.

claims.claim: A global currency will be implemented to control finances.
claims.evidence: Discussions about digital currencies and the potential for a global financial system are interpreted as a plot to eliminate national currencies.
claims.criticism: The development of digital currencies and discussions about global financial stability are driven by economic factors and technological advancements, not a conspiracy to control the world.

keyFigures.name: David Icke
keyFigures.role: Popularized the theory, linking it to reptilian overlords.

keyFigures.name: Carroll Quigley
keyFigures.role: Historian whose work is often misinterpreted to support the theory.

keyFigures.name: Michael Barkun
keyFigures.role: Academic who studies conspiracy theories and their cultural impact.

mythologyConnections: Babylonian Tower of Babel (attempt at unified power)
Biblical prophecies of a global Antichrist kingdom

timeline.event: Founding of the Illuminati, often cited as the origin of the OWG plot.
Formation of the League of Nations, seen by some as a precursor to global government.
Establishment of the United Nations, frequently targeted as a tool for global control.
Rise of globalization and international organizations, fueling OWG anxieties.

sources.author: Michael Barkun
sources.work: A Culture of Conspiracy: Apocalyptic Visions in Contemporary America
sources.title: Michael Barkun
